skills.title: Determining the Task (Task 1 Academic)
skills.note: Initial Phase. Analyze the chart, graph, table, or map to identify what key information you must summarize.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Read the prompt carefully to see what graphic is presented (bar chart, line graph, pie chart, table, or diagram).
  • Identify the time period (past, present, or future) to select the correct grammatical verb tenses.
  • Look for the units of measurement (percentages, millions of dollars, tons) to report figures accurately.

items.q: Prompt: 'The bar chart shows average spending per pupil in 2000-2015.' What is the task and tense?
items.a: Task: Summarize the changes in spending per pupil. Tense: Past tense (since years 2000 to 2015 are in the past). Use verbs like 'decreased', 'rose'.

skills.title: Determining the Topic (Task 1 Academic)
skills.note: Formulates the Overview. Define the main subject, variables, and categories of the graphic to paraphrase in your intro.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Determine the topic by looking at the graphic's title and axis labels.
  • Paraphrase the prompt using synonyms to write your first introduction sentence.

items.q: Paraphrase: 'The table shows the percentage of students passing a math test in three different schools.'
items.a: Replace 'table shows': 'The table illustrates...' Replace 'percentage of students passing': '...the proportion of pupils who successfuly passed...' Paraphrased: 'The table illustrates the proportion of pupils who successfully passed a mathematics examination across three distinct educational institutions.'

skills.title: Making Comparisons (Task 1 Academic)
skills.note: Scores Task Achievement. Group similar data together and highlight the highest, lowest, or most dramatic trends.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Do not list every data point sequentially. Group data into high, medium, and low categories.
  • Use comparative structures (*higher than, double, in contrast, similarly*).

items.q: Chart data: Cafeteria spending was $50 per student in 2000, and fell to $10 in 2015. Write a comparison sentence.
items.a: Compare start and end: $50 in 2000 vs $10 in 2015. Use comparative verbs: 'fell dramatically', 'a five-fold decrease'. Write: 'Spending on cafeteria services fell dramatically from $50 per student in 2000 to a mere $10 in 2015, representing a five-fold decrease.'

skills.title: Making an Outline (Task 1 Academic)
skills.note: Organizes Paragraph Structure. Plan a 4-paragraph layout: 1) Introduction, 2) Overview, 3) Body 1, 4) Body 2.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Never start writing without planning. Spend 2-3 minutes outlining.

items.q: Create an outline structure for a line graph showing sales of books and e-books over 10 years.
items.a: Para 1: Paraphrase graph title. Para 2: Overview (e-books increased, physical books decreased overall). Para 3: Detailed data for physical books. Para 4: Detailed data for e-books.

skills.title: Writing the Introduction (Task 1 Academic)
skills.note: First Impression. Paraphrase the prompt using synonyms and state what the graphic compares.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Paraphrase the original sentence. Replace words like *gives information about* with *provides data on*.

items.q: Prompt: 'The chart shows the number of tourists visiting three different cities in Europe from 2010 to 2020.' Write the introduction.
items.a: Paraphrase nouns and verbs: 'chart shows' -> 'bar chart compares'. Paraphrase time: 'from 2010 to 2020' -> 'over a decade starting in 2010'. Introduction: 'The bar chart compares the volume of international tourists who visited three distinct European cities over a decade starting in 2010.'

skills.title: Writing the Paragraphs (Task 1 Academic)
skills.note: Body Paragraph Development. Report detailed data points, values, and trends accurately with cohesive transition markers.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Use transition words to compare data groups (*in terms of, regarding, as for, on the other hand*).

items.q: Write a body paragraph sentence stating that school library spending was at 15% in 2000, then rose to 35% in 2010.
items.a: State initial value: 'library spending stood at 15% in 2000'. State change: 'before rising significantly'. Combine: 'Regarding school expenditures, funding allocated for library resources stood at 15% in 2000, before rising significantly to hit 35% in 2010.'

skills.title: Writing the Conclusion (Task 1 Academic)
skills.note: Completes Task 1. Restate the main overview trend clearly and briefly in 1-2 final sentences.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Summarize the core takeaways without introducing new numbers or data points.

items.q: Write a concluding sentence for a chart showing a transition from fossil fuels to renewable energy.
items.a: Identify major shift: Fossil fuels down, renewables up. Avoid numbers. Write: 'In conclusion, the data shows a major shift in national power generation, with clean energy sources gradually replacing fossil fuels over the period.'

skills.title: Determining the Task (Task 1 General Training)
skills.note: For GT Letters. Identify if the letter is formal, semi-formal, or informal, and check the 3 bullet points you must cover.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Determine the letter tone: Formal (to a landlord, employer, company), Informal (to a friend).

items.q: Prompt: 'Write a letter to your landlord complaining about a leak. Explain the problem, tell him what needs fixing, and request action.' Determine the tone and opening.
items.a: Tone: Formal (communication with landlord). Opening: 'Dear Mr. [Landlord's Surname]' or 'Dear Sir/Madam'. Statement of purpose: 'I am writing to bring to your attention a serious water leakage problem in my apartment.'

skills.title: Determining the Topic (Task 1 General Training)
skills.note: Brainstorming context. Establish the background story (who, what, where) to make the letter sound natural and cohesive.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Create a believable background story to build your points on.

items.q: Brainstorm details for: 'Write a letter to a manager complaining about bad customer service at a restaurant.'
items.a: Identify who: Waiter was rude. Identify what: Food served cold, waiter ignored requests. Identify where/when: Friday night at the downtown branch. Use these details to build your letter paragraph.

skills.title: Brainstorming Ideas (Task 1 General Training)
skills.note: Letter Details. Generate specific request details, dates, and names for each of the letter's 3 prompts.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Generate realistic details for your requests: dates, exact times, items, prices.

items.q: Write a request sentence to a company asking for a full refund for a broken laptop.
items.a: State the request clearly: 'ask for a full refund'. Keep it formal: 'request a complete reimbursement'. Write: 'Therefore, I request a complete reimbursement of the purchase price, which stands at $1,200, as soon as possible.'

skills.title: Making an Outline (Task 1 General Training)
skills.note: GT Letter Structure. Outline a 5-part layout: 1) Salutation, 2) Purpose, 3) Bullet Points coverage, 4) Sign-off.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Structure the letter logically, dedicating a paragraph to each bullet point.

items.q: Draft the outline of an informal letter inviting a friend to a weekend camping trip.
items.a: Salutation: 'Dear Mark,'. Purpose: Invite him to camp next weekend. Details: Explain location (Rocky Mountains), packing list, travel plan. Sign-off: 'Warmly,' or 'Best wishes,' followed by your name.

skills.title: Writing the Introduction (Task 1 General Training)
skills.note: Letter Opening. Learn the standard opening phrases to state your letter's objective immediately.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • State the purpose of writing in the first sentence.

items.q: Write a formal introduction for a letter requesting a change of work shift.
items.a: State the action: 'I am writing to request...'. State the details: 'change my current shift assignment'. Write: 'I am writing to formally request a transfer from my current night shift assignment to the morning shift, starting next month.'

skills.title: Writing the Paragraphs (Task 1 General Training)
skills.note: GT Body development. Use appropriate formal or informal cohesive links to detail your story.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Use cohesive devices appropriate for the letter's tone.

items.q: Write a complaint paragraph detailing a broken heating system in winter.
items.a: State the problem: Heater broke down. Explain consequence: Flat is freezing, unsafe for kids. Write: 'The central heating system broke down last Friday. Consequently, temperatures inside the flat have dropped to unsafe levels, making it freezing and uninhabitable for my children.'

skills.title: Writing the Conclusion (Task 1 General Training)
skills.note: Letter Closing. Request a specific action or response, and sign off using the correct greeting.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Request response or action, then close appropriately.

items.q: Write a concluding paragraph for a complaint letter to a hotel manager.
items.a: Request action: Refund or compensation. Polite closing: 'appreciate your attention'. Write: 'I appreciate your prompt attention to this matter and hope to receive a full refund shortly. Yours sincerely, [Name].'

skills.title: Determining the Task (Task 2 Essay)
skills.note: Essay Core. Identify the essay type: Agree/Disagree, Discussion (Both views), Problem/Solution, or Double Question.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Identify task type to structure your body paragraphs accordingly.

items.q: Prompt: 'Some people think university education should be free. Others argue that students should pay tuition. Discuss both views.' Determine the essay type and task.
items.a: Type: Discussion + Opinion essay. Task: Write one body paragraph supporting free education, one body paragraph supporting paid tuition, and state your own opinion clearly.

skills.title: Developing a Thesis Statement (Task 2)
skills.note: Scores Coherence & Cohesion. Write a 1-sentence answer to the essay prompt to present your position clearly.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Formulate a clear thesis expressing your opinion and core reasoning.

items.q: Write a thesis statement for: 'Should homework be banned in schools?' (Position: Ban it).
items.a: Identify position: Ban homework. State the reason: Causes stress, limits free time. Thesis: 'I believe that school homework should be completely banned because it causes excessive stress for young students and reduces their necessary leisure time.'

skills.title: Organizing Your Writing (Task 2)
skills.note: Essay Layout. Plan a 4-paragraph layout: 1) Intro, 2) Body 1 (argument A), 3) Body 2 (argument B), 4) Conclusion.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Spend 2 minutes outline writing before drafting to ensure paragraph balance.

items.q: Draft the outline of an Agree/Disagree essay about whether public transport should be free.
items.a: Para 1: Intro + thesis (strongly agree). Para 2: Body 1 (environmental benefits - cuts emissions). Para 3: Body 3 (social benefits - helps low-income workers). Para 4: Conclusion (restate position).

skills.title: Writing the Introduction (Task 2)
skills.note: Essay Opening. Paraphrase the prompt in 1-2 sentences and end with your clear thesis statement.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Introduce the topic generally, then state your thesis.

items.q: Write an introduction for: 'Governments should invest in art rather than science.' (Disagree).
items.a: Paraphrase topic: It is often argued whether state funding should prioritize artistic projects over scientific research. Thesis: I disagree and believe scientific funding is far more critical for society. Intro: 'It is often argued that state budgets should prioritize the arts over scientific research. In my opinion, I strongly disagree with this view, as scientific advancements play a far more vital role in national development.'

skills.title: Writing the Paragraphs (Task 2)
skills.note: Body Paragraph Logic. Build each paragraph using the PEEL method: Point, Explanation, Example, Link.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Follow the PEEL structure for logical coherence and clarity.

items.q: Write a body paragraph point showing that online education is flexible, using PEEL.
items.a: Point: Online learning is highly flexible. Explanation: Students can study at their own pace. Example: For instance, working professionals can watch recorded lectures at night. Link: This flexibility leads to higher access to higher education.

skills.title: Writing the Conclusion (Task 2)
skills.note: Completes Essay. Summarize the main points and restate your thesis in 2 final sentences.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Summarize main arguments without introducing new claims.

items.q: Write a conclusion for an essay arguing that technology has made us more isolated.
items.a: Start with transition: 'In conclusion...'. Summarize: Technology limits physical interactions. Write: 'In conclusion, it is clear that while digital devices connect us globally, they often make us isolated locally. I believe that moderation is key to maintaining healthy face-to-face relationships.'

templates.name: Template 1: Agree / Disagree (Opinion) Essay Outline
templates.structure:
Introduction:
1. Paraphrase Statement: State the controversial topic in your own words (e.g., 'Some assert that...').
2. Thesis Statement: State your clear position (e.g., 'I completely agree with this proposal because...').

Body Paragraph 1 (Reason 1):
- Point: State the first reason supporting your position.
- Explain: Elaborate on how/why this is the case.
- Example: Give a concrete instance or study proving your point.
- Link: Re-align back to your thesis.

Body Paragraph 2 (Reason 2):
- Point: State the second reason (e.g., 'Furthermore, it is clear that...').
- Explain: Elaborate and provide logical support.
- Example: Give a factual example.
- Link: Re-align.

Conclusion:
- Restatement: Summarize the two main points in one sentence.
- Final Stance: Firmly reiterate your overall opinion.

templates.name: Template 2: Discussion (Discuss Both Views + Opinion) Outline
templates.structure:
Introduction:
1. Paraphrase Statement: Acknowledge that people have differing views on the topic.
2. Thesis Statement: State your own position and briefly outline both sides.

Body Paragraph 1 (View A - Opposing side):
- Point: Explain why some people support the first view.
- Detail: Provide their logical reasons.
- Example: Highlight an example of View A in practice.

Body Paragraph 2 (View B - Your supported side):
- Point: Explain the arguments for the second view.
- Detail: Explain why this view is stronger.
- Example: Provide an example showing the benefits.

Conclusion:
- Summary: Re-state that both sides have valid points, but firmly conclude in favor of your opinion.

templates.name: Template 3: Task 1 Academic Line Graph / Chart Report
templates.structure:
Paragraph 1: Introduction
- Sentence: Paraphrase the graph prompt (e.g., 'The line graph depicts the volume of... between [Year] and [Year] in [Location].'). Include units.

Paragraph 2: Overview
- Sentence 1: Identify the main overall trend (e.g., 'Overall, the sales of [Product A] experienced an upward trend, whereas [Product B] dropped.').
- Sentence 2: Highlight the peak or the most dramatic change. *Do not write specific numbers here.*

Paragraph 3: Body Paragraph 1 (Category A Detail)
- Report starting numbers, progress, and end points for the first category. Group similar trends together.

Paragraph 4: Body Paragraph 2 (Category B Detail)
- Contrast the first category with the second category. Mention exact numbers and years in brackets or using 'stood at' or 'rose to'.

templates.name: Template 4: Task 1 GT Formal Complaint Letter Layout
templates.structure:
Salutation: Dear Sir or Madam, (or Dear Mr./Ms. [Surname] if known)

Paragraph 1: Purpose of the letter
- State clearly why you are writing (e.g., 'I am writing to bring to your attention a serious issue regarding...').

Paragraph 2: Detailed explanation of the problem (Bullet 1 & 2)
- Outline the exact problem, when it occurred, and how it is affecting you (e.g., 'The central heating system has failed, resulting in freezing...').

Paragraph 3: Proposed solution and action request (Bullet 3)
- State what action you expect (e.g., 'Therefore, I request that a technician be dispatched immediately...').

Sign-off & Closing:
- I look forward to your prompt response.
- Yours faithfully, (use if starting Dear Sir/Madam) OR Yours sincerely, (use if you know their name)
- [Your Name]

samples.title: Sample 1 (Task 2 Essay): Free University Tuition
samples.prompt: Some people think that university education should be free for all students. Discuss both views and give your opinion.

6.5.essay:
[ Band 6.5 Model Essay ]
In many countries today, people discuss if university should be free. Some think it is a good idea, while other people say that students should pay. In this essay I will discuss both sides and give my opinion.

First, if university is free, it helps poor students who want to study. But it costs the government a lot of moneySimplistic repetitive vocabulary. Universities need money for libraries and computers. If there is no money, the quality of study will go down. For example, in some poor countries, colleges have old libraries because they do not have money.

Second, free universityRepetitive subject reference makes people get better jobs. When poor people get jobs, they pay taxes. So the government gets money back. But some students do not study hard if they do not pay. They just waste time.

In conclusion, I think university should be free because it is fair. But the government must check that students study hard.
6.5.commentary:
Band 6.5 Evaluation & Feedback:
  • Task Response: Discusses both sides but arguments are basic and lack development. Stance is clear but simple.
  • Coherence & Cohesion: Cohesive markers are very repetitive (*First, Second, In conclusion*). Paragraphs are too short and disconnected.
  • Lexical Resource: Basic vocabulary (*poor people, a lot of money, waste time*). Multiple repetitions of *free university* and *money*.
  • Grammar: Mostly simple sentences. Minor grammatical slip-ups, though generally understandable.

7.5.essay:
[ Band 7.5 Model Essay ]
It is widely discussed whether tertiary education should be fully funded by governments or paid for by students. In my view, although running universities requires heavy capital, providing free tuition offers significant social and economic advantages.

On the one hand, tuition fees ensure that universities are well-funded. When students pay, institutions have more capital to invest in top-tier research facilities and recruit high-quality professors. For instance, in countries with high tuition like the US, universities dominate global research rankings. However, this system excludes talented low-income youth.Good contrast but lacks advanced vocabulary

On the other hand, free university education promotes equal opportunity. If access depends solely on academic merit rather than financial wealth, society benefits from a highly skilled workforce. For example, Scandinavian countries offer free tuition and have high levels of innovation.

In conclusion, I believe that university education should be free, as the long-term societal and economic benefits are very bigSlight lexical drop in conclusion compared to the funding costs.
7.5.commentary:
Band 7.5 Evaluation & Feedback:
  • Task Response: Effectively discusses both viewpoints and presents a clear stance. Arguments are logically supported.
  • Coherence & Cohesion: Good paragraph progression. Standard transitions are used correctly (*On the one hand, On the other hand, In conclusion*).
  • Lexical Resource: Strong vocabulary (*tertiary education, dominate, innovation*), but drops slightly in the conclusion (*are very big*).
  • Grammar: Good mix of complex sentences with only minor slips.

9.0.essay:
[ Band 9.0 Model Essay ]
It is often argued whether higher education should be fully funded by governments or paid for by the students themselves. In my opinion, while tertiary education is expensive, making it free is crucial for social mobility and economic development.

On the one handCohesive paragraph transition, tuition fees ensure that universities are well-funded. When students pay, institutions have more capital to invest in top-tier research facilities and recruit high-quality professors. For instance, in countries with high tuition like the US, universities dominate global research rankings. However, this fee-paying system inevitably excludes talented low-income youthSophisticated argument contrast, perpetuating social inequality.

On the other handCohesive paragraph contrast, free university education promotes equal opportunity. If access depends solely on academic merit rather than financial wealth, society benefits from a highly skilled workforce. For example, Scandinavian countries offer free tuition and have high levels of innovation. To sum upFormal conclusion signpost, I believe that university education should be free, as the long-term societal and economic benefits far outweigh the funding costs.
9.0.commentary:
Band 9.0 Evaluation & Feedback:
  • Task Response: Flawless task coverage. Position is fully developed and sustained throughout the essay.
  • Coherence & Cohesion: Natural, seamless flow. Linking words are sophisticated and never feel forced.
  • Lexical Resource: Excellent lexical range (*perpetuating, tertiary education, social mobility, merit-based*).
  • Grammar: Highly varied sentence structures showing full flexibility and precision.

samples.title: Sample 2 (Task 1 Academic): Line Graph - Meat & Fish Consumption
samples.prompt: The line graph shows the weekly consumption of fish and different kinds of meat in a European country between 1979 and 2004.

6.5.essay:
[ Band 6.5 Model Essay ]
The line graph shows how much beef, chicken, pork, and fish people ate in a European country from 1979 to 2004.

First, beef consumption was very high at 220 grams in 1979, but then it went downSimplistic verb for decline to only 100 grams in 2004. Next, chicken started at 145 grams and went up a lotSimplistic verb for growth to 250 grams in 2004. It became the most popular meat.

Pork and fish did not change much. Pork started at 75 grams and ended at 75 grams. Fish was the lowest at 55 grams in 1979 and ended at 48 grams in 2004.

Overall, beef went down, chicken went up, and fish and pork stayed the same.
6.5.commentary:
Band 6.5 Evaluation & Feedback:
  • Task Achievement: Details are reported line-by-line rather than comparing trends. The overview at the end is very basic.
  • Coherence: Paragraphs are separated, but transitions are simple (*First, Next, Overall*).
  • Lexical Resource: Simplistic verbs (*went down, went up a lot, stayed the same*). Repetitive phrasing.
  • Grammar: Relies on simple sentences. Preposition usage is correct but basic.

7.5.essay:
[ Band 7.5 Model Essay ]
The line graph illustrates the weekly consumption of beef, chicken, pork, and fish in a European nation between 1979 and 2004.

Overall, the consumption of beef experienced a significant decline, whereas chicken consumption grew dramatically. In contrast, pork and fish remained relatively stable.

Beef consumption was the highest in 1979 at 220 grams, before decreasing steadily to 100 grams in 2004. Conversely, chicken consumption steadily climbedGood comparative transition, starting at 145 grams and overtaking beef in 1989 to reach a peak of 250 grams in 2004.

Pork consumption fluctuated slightly, starting at 75 grams and finishing at the same level. Fish remained the least consumed item, showing a minor drop from 55 grams to 48 grams.
7.5.commentary:
Band 7.5 Evaluation & Feedback:
  • Task Achievement: Clear overview of the main trends. Key features are compared successfully.
  • Coherence: Paragraphs are logical and data is grouped well (meat drops vs rises, stable items).
  • Lexical Resource: Strong vocabulary (*illustrates, fluctuated, overtook, consumption*).
  • Grammar: Mix of complex structures, though a bit formulaic.

9.0.essay:
[ Band 9.0 Model Essay ]
The line graph depicts the consumption of three types of meat and fish in a European nation over a 25-year period starting in 1979.

Overall, beef consumption experienced a dramatic decline, whereas chicken consumption surged to become the most popular protein sourcePrecise descriptions of trend crossover by the end of the period. Meanwhile, pork and fish consumption remained relatively stable.

In 1979, beef was the most consumed item, standing at 220 grams per weekAdvanced prepositional structure. However, it dropped sharply over the years to finish at just 100 grams in 2004. Conversely, chicken consumption steadily climbed, overtaking beef in 1989Accurate pinpointing of chart intersection and peaking at 250 grams by the end of the timeline.

Regarding pork and fish, their numbers remained comparatively low. Pork consumption fluctuated marginally around 75 to 90 grams, while fish saw a slight decrease from 55 grams to end the period at 48 grams.
9.0.commentary:
Band 9.0 Evaluation & Feedback:
  • Task Achievement: Outstanding summary of data trends. Selects key crossover years (1989) and makes flawless comparisons.
  • Coherence & Cohesion: Information is organized perfectly into logical comparisons. Transitions are varied (*Conversely, Meanwhile, Regarding*).
  • Lexical Resource: Precise vocabulary (*depicts, surged, marginally, standing at*).
  • Grammar: Flawless grammatical range, using participle clauses and complex comparisons.

samples.title: Sample 3 (Task 1 GT): Formal Letter - Kitchen Water Leak
samples.prompt: Write a letter to your landlord complaining about a water leak in your apartment. Explain the problem, tell him what needs fixing, and request action.

6.5.essay:
[ Band 6.5 Model Letter ]
Dear landlord,

I am writing to tell you that there is a big leak in my kitchen. It started yesterday when it was raining. The water is dripping from the ceiling and making a mess on the floor. I cannot use my stove to cook.

Also, the plaster is cracking. I had to turn off the lights because I am scared of electricity problems. It is a dangerous thingSimplistic phrase for hazard for me.

Please send a plumber to fix this quickly. I need the kitchen to be repaired soon.

Yours sincerely,
James Kingston
6.5.commentary:
Band 6.5 Evaluation & Feedback:
  • Task Achievement: Addresses all prompts but in a very brief way. The tone is formal in the sign-off but informal in the salutation (*Dear landlord*).
  • Coherence: Layout has paragraphs, but ideas are simple.
  • Lexical Resource: Basic vocabulary (*big leak, mess, dangerous thing*).
  • Grammar: Mostly simple sentences. Minor awkward phrasing (*making a mess, a dangerous thing*).

7.5.essay:
[ Band 7.5 Model Letter ]
Dear Mr. Davies,

I am writing to inform you about a serious water leak in my kitchen. The leak started yesterday afternoon after a heavy storm. Water is dripping from the ceiling onto the stove, which makes the kitchen unusable.

Additionally, the plaster on the ceiling is starting to crack and peel. I have turned off the lights to avoid any electrical problems. This is quite dangerous.

Therefore, I would like to ask you to send a plumber to repair the leak as soon as possible. I also want to get the ceiling repainted after it is fixed.

Yours sincerely,
James Kingston
7.5.commentary:
Band 7.5 Evaluation & Feedback:
  • Task Achievement: Letter fully covers all points. Tone is polite and appropriately formal.
  • Coherence: Good logical structure and separation of issues.
  • Lexical Resource: Good vocabulary (*inform you, unusable, additionally, plaster*).
  • Grammar: Grammatical structures are correct with few errors.

9.0.essay:
[ Band 9.0 Model Letter ]
Dear Mr. Davies,

I am writing to bring to your immediate attentionPolite formal complaint opening a serious water leakage issue in the kitchen of my apartment, which is Unit 4B.

The leak began yesterday afternoon, following heavy rainfall. Water is actively dripping from the ceiling directly above the stove area, causing significant pooling on the floor. FurthermoreCohesive marker adding secondary detail, the dampness is causing the plaster on the ceiling to crack and peel, which poses a safety hazard. I have had to turn off the overhead light fixtures in the kitchen to prevent an electrical short-circuit.

ThereforeTransition stating request action, I request that you dispatch a professional plumber to repair the roof or pipes immediately to prevent further structural damage. I also request your approval to have the kitchen ceiling replastered once the leak is sealed.

I look forward to your prompt response and action on this matter.

Yours sincerely,
James Kingston
9.0.commentary:
Band 9.0 Evaluation & Feedback:
  • Task Achievement: Fully answers all points with perfect, highly persuasive formal tone.
  • Coherence: Seamless linking of ideas across paragraphs.
  • Lexical Resource: Sophisticated, precise vocabulary (*bring to your attention, safety hazard, dispatch, structural damage*).
  • Grammar: Perfect grammatical complexity and execution.
